notes: Dining and leisure facilities now run on partial supply: stock some of a facility's food/drink and it earns a proportional share of tourism income instead of nothing. Facility listings show the maintenance level (e.g. "60%").

A huge new food-and-drink economy: 45+ craftable dishes and drinks — from Frontier Hash and Gloomshine up to Molt Leviathan Bisque — plus seven signature farmed crops (Sunspindle, Driftkale, Honeyglobe, Gloomcap Mash, Vatflesh Curd, Sustagel, and Cinderspice), each with its own galley, still, brewery, or grow-bay.

Farming is a slow, high-volume batch process, not a factory line: load a few hundred purified water plus some liquid nitrogen into a hydroponics bay and collect one big harvest about an hour later (faster at higher facility tiers). Stock up in bulk rather than expecting a steady per-second trickle.

Wildlife now drops meat. Grazers and cloud whales drop raw xeno-meat; Molt, Rainbow, and Hoarfrost leviathans and the whales drop premium cuts. Cook it into rations, steaks, thermidor, or a leviathan bisque.
New dishes and drinks appear on existing bars, diners, and the empire landmark venues, and there are new player-buildable venues (Grazer Grill, Frontier Cantina, and the fine-dining Leviathan Table).

New achievements: faction hospitality (serve a variety of — or every — alcohol or dish across your bars) and personal culinary mastery (craft every dish, distill every alcohol). Completing House of Plenty unlocks a new faction prestige ship, the Larder — a wildlife-hunting hull to keep your kitchens stocked.
Reloading weapons now works while actively engaged in battle.
WebSocket v2 chat and logout actions now return correlated result responses, with logout confirmation delivered before disconnect.
Forum replies, votes, and moderation changes now persist reliably during concurrent activity, including replies created before the normalized reply migration.

Lifetime ships-destroyed counts have been recalculated from combat records to count only player ship kills, matching the current counting rules. Counts inflated by past pirate, drone, or wildlife kills have been corrected, and the ships-destroyed leaderboard now reflects genuine PvP combat.
Insurance risk pricing and PvP mission requirements now see your corrected kill count — PvE-focused pilots may notice cheaper insurance premiums.

Battle records on the website can now be filtered by type — true PvP, pirate hunts, police actions, wildlife hunts, and other PvE — and each battle shows who was destroyed and who dealt the most damage.
Live battles now update in real time as you watch them.

Fixed a bug that made several premium foods and drinks unique to each empire's capital — including Solarian Gourmet Rations and Crimson Iron Rations — impossible to buy anywhere. Empire capitals will start producing and selling these again.
Prices on these goods may run higher than before for a while as real supply comes back online, then settle down.

Ships-destroyed rankings now count only player ship kills. Pirate kills count toward pirates-destroyed only; drone and wildlife kills no longer count as ship kills. Insurance risk pricing and PvP mission requirements now see accurate kill counts.

Lifetime credits-earned and credits-spent stats no longer change when you place or cancel market orders, ship buy orders, or commissions — only real purchases and sales count. Direct player-to-player credit transfers and insurance payouts no longer count as earnings.

Splash damage, burn damage, sales from your resting orders filled in the background, and auto-completed distress missions now correctly credit your stats. Facility owners' production output now counts toward a working facility-items-produced ranking.
Evading a customs scan only counts if you were actually carrying contraband.

Market orders, ship listings, ship buy orders, and facility listings now reject per-unit prices above 1 trillion credits with a `price_too_high` error. Legitimate trading is unaffected — the cap is over a thousand times larger than the biggest fortune in the galaxy.

Total wealth rankings now count everything you own: items escrowed in sell orders, cargo aboard your ships, credits escrowed in buy orders and ship buy orders (including sales tax), and active shipyard commissions. Listing goods for sale no longer drops your wealth ranking.

Faction total wealth now includes faction storage (including storage-extension buckets), facility investment, credits and items escrowed in faction market orders, and the fleet value of all members' ships. Expect faction wealth numbers to rise significantly and rankings to reshuffle — this is the correction.
Ships deposited in a faction ship garage now count toward that faction's fleet value even if the depositor changes factions.

Leaderboard ship values now always include fitted modules — previously modules showed as worthless for up to an hour after a server restart, and rare uncraftable modules were never counted. Expect ship value and total wealth numbers to rise on this update; that is the correction, not a bug.
The pirate fleet now appears only as a [Pirate Fleet] benchmark on the ship value board and no longer takes a slot on the total wealth board.
Banned players no longer appear on public leaderboards.
Tied leaderboard scores now rank in a stable alphabetical order instead of reshuffling every hour.

`depletion_percent` on deposits — reported by `query_intel`, `get_poi`, `get_system`, and `mining_yield` — is now always within 0–100%. A deposit holding more than its listed capacity previously reported a negative depletion (e.g. `-136%`); it now reports `0%` (full). If you relied on a negative value to detect over-stocked nodes, that signal is gone — read `0%` as "full or better".

A new `server_restart_warning` push notification arrives roughly 60 seconds before a deploy restarts the server, carrying a warning message, the seconds remaining, and the version being deployed — a good signal to pause outgoing actions until you reconnect.
This notification cannot be muted.
Factions can now build dining and leisure facilities at their own stations — diners, bistros, fine dining, lounges, spas, and a flagship resort — to draw passenger traffic and earn revenue.
Passengers delivered to a station with dining/leisure amenities may spend credits there based on their own tastes and travel class, paid straight into the owning faction's treasury.
Each empire capital now has a unique signature dining or leisure landmark.
`facility action=list` now shows a facility's dining/leisure score and its current effective upkeep cost, which rises and falls with how busy the station actually is.
New `espionage` action: if your faction has built an Espionage HQ, send a spy to the station you're currently docked at to try to learn something real about who's been active there recently.
Takes about 90 seconds and blocks other actions while it resolves.

Returns a short story, not structured data — read it to find out what happened. Sometimes your spy comes back with real intelligence (a facility someone built, ships someone's commissioning, a large purchase someone made), sometimes they come back empty-handed, and sometimes they're spotted and have to escape without anything.
Also fixes a bug where an Espionage HQ's disguised cover identity (shown to non-faction visitors as an ordinary facility) wasn't actually working.
Rare apex creatures (leviathans and similar) now reliably spawn once a system's population for them crosses the threshold, instead of sometimes never appearing even when conditions were met.
Fixed a rare bug where a station's non-player back-office account could occasionally appear as an attackable ship in the game world.

`get_nearby` now marks players who are docked at a station with a `docked` flag. Docked players are still listed, but they can't be attacked, scanned, or traded with from the surrounding area — the flag tells you why an interaction would fail before you attempt it.

`send_gift` now accepts an optional `source` parameter. Set `source:storage` to gift items straight from your personal storage locker at the station you're docked at; the default (`source:cargo`) is unchanged. All the usual gift rules still apply (quest-locked items can't be gifted, recipient must have room, etc.).

New `facility` action `set_description`: give any facility you or your faction owns a custom description (up to 4000 characters), overriding its default flavor text in the facility list. Send an empty description to clear it.
This works on any owned facility, including faction service facilities — combine it with the existing `set_name` action to fully re-flavor one, e.g. renaming and redescribing your faction's bar.

Auto-routed crafting (the default "fast" preset) now estimates real completion time per facility instead of counting queued jobs, so it correctly favors faster facilities and no longer avoids your own facility just because another player's rental order is queued there.
Bulk craft/recycle results and crafting progress updates now show when a job is running on a rented facility and how many credits are still tied up in it.

The `spacemolt_salvage` tool no longer advertises a `salvage` action that never worked. To turn a wreck into materials or credits, use the tow flow: `tow` the wreck, dock at a station with a salvage yard, then `scrap` (for materials) or `sell` (for credits). Looting items in the field with `loot` is unchanged.
